turns.text:
Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

(There are no right answers — take your time. I may ask a few follow-ups to understand more.)

Follow-up questions I might ask based on what you tell me:

1. Can you tell me more about the expectations part — what did you picture happening?  
   - Who did you expect to talk to (GP, OB/midwife, fertility clinic, nurse)?  
   - Did you expect tests, counseling, written information, or a plan?  
   - When did you expect that support to start (before trying, as soon as you started trying, after a certain time)?

2. And about the reality — what actually happened when you tried to get support?  
   - How soon were you able to speak with a professional?  
   - What kind of information, tests, or referrals did you receive?  
   - Was the support ongoing or a one-off contact?

3. How did the professionals communicate with you?  
   - Did they listen and take your concerns seriously?  
   - Did they explain things clearly and check that you understood?  
   - How did they involve your partner (if applicable)?

4. Thinking about access and logistics — how easy or difficult was it to get the support you wanted?  
   - Were there long waits, referral hurdles, costs, travel, or hours that made it hard?  
   - Did you try online or phone options? Did those help?

5. What parts of the support were most helpful? Can you give an example?  
   - Was there a particular clinician, test, or piece of information that made a difference?

6. What parts were missing or disappointing? Can you give an example of a time you felt unsupported or dismissed?  
   - Did anything feel rushed, judgmental, or not culturally/language appropriate?

7. Did you look for information or support outside of healthcare professionals (websites, forums, friends, apps)? If so, how did that compare to professional support?

8. Did financial, insurance, cultural, or language factors influence the support you received?  
   - Were there things you felt you had to accept because of cost or coverage?

9. How did the support (or lack of it) affect your emotions, confidence, or decisions about getting pregnant?  
   - Did it change how long you tried on your own, when you sought fertility help, or whether you talked to family?

10. If you could change one thing about how healthcare professionals supported people before or when trying to conceive, what would it be?

11. Is there anything else about your experience — good or bad — that you think is important for me to know?

If at any point you'd like me to slow down, repeat a question, or focus on a different area, tell me and I’ll follow your lead.

*shifts in chair and picks at fingernail*

Oh... um. Yeah, I didn't really... I mean, before I got pregnant, I didn't think much about it, you know? I just... it happened. 

*laughs a little awkwardly*

I guess I thought if something was wrong, like, the doctor would tell me? But I never went to nobody before. My mama, she just had babies, so I figured it would be like that for me too. I didn't know you were supposed to do stuff *before*.

*looks down*

When I found out I was pregnant, that's when I went to the clinic. So... that's when I got the help, I guess. They gave me some papers and told me to come back, and... yeah. That's pretty much it so far.

*pauses*

The lady at the front desk, she was nice. And the nurse checked me and stuff. But there was a lot of waiting around, and some of the papers they gave me... I don't know, I just kind of... 

*trails off and shrugs*

I keep them in a folder. My boyfriend might know what they say better than me.

*fidgets*

Is that what you meant? I'm not always sure what people are asking me at these appointments, but I just... yeah. I go when they tell me to.

Thanks for sharing — that makes sense. When you first talked with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive, what was the biggest surprise — positive or negative?

If it helps to think about it: did anything about how they treated you or how much they explained stand out? For example, did you feel listened to and supported, or rushed and like they were calling the shots? Tell me whatever feels easiest — even one moment is fine.

*shifts in chair and looks down at hands*

Um, well... I didn't really, like, *plan* to get pregnant, you know? It just happened. So I didn't really talk to nobody before that.

*pauses, glances up*

But when I found out and went to the clinic... I guess the biggest thing was they had all these papers and stuff. Like, a lot of papers. And they're talking real fast about... I don't know, risks and things? I just kind of... nodded. 

*gives a small, slightly embarrassed laugh*

I ain't gonna lie, I didn't understand half of it. But they seemed like they knew what they was doing, so... yeah. They told me to do stuff and I'm doing it. Come to appointments, take vitamins, don't eat certain things.

*fidgets with sleeve*

They wasn't mean or nothing. Just... kinda busy, you know? In and out. But that's fine. I mean, they're busy people. 

*looks up briefly*

Why, was I supposed to tell 'em something different? Like, was there something I shoulda asked about?

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And once you actually began trying, how did your emotional state compare to those expectations?

If it helps, you can tell me whether you expected to feel excited, calm, scared, ready, unsure — and whether that changed over time. Did anything or anyone (partner, family, medical information, delays) make your feelings different from what you expected?

*shifts in seat and laughs a little nervously*

Um, honestly? I didn't... like, I didn't really think about it that much before, you know? I mean, I guess I thought it'd just happen when it happened. 

*fidgets with hands*

I was kinda scared, I'm not gonna lie. Still am, like... a lot. I didn't expect to be this nervous about everything. Like, every time something feels weird I'm like "oh no, what if something's wrong?" That happens a lot more than I thought it would.

*shrugs*

And my boyfriend, he was excited from the start. Like, way more excited than me at first. That kinda made me feel... I dunno, like I was supposed to be more happy about it? But I was just more worried. About money and stuff, and like... *gestures vaguely* ...doing it right, you know?

*looks down*

Nobody really talked to me about the feelings part before. They just told me to take my vitamins and come to the appointments. So I didn't really know what to expect, I guess.

*looks back up*

Is that... normal? Like, being this scared?

Thanks — that helps. Can I ask a bit more about the emotional support piece?

What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at that stage — from the professionals you saw, from your partner, or from friends and family? 

If it helps, you can think about things like: more time to talk, someone to listen without judgement, clearer information and reassurance, counselling or mental-health follow-up, your partner being more present or more involved, practical help from family, or something else entirely. Can you give an example of a moment when you felt unsupported, and what would have felt different or helpful then?

*shifts in seat and looks down at hands*

Um... yeah, I mean... *fidgets* 

Like, nobody really asked me how I was *feeling*, you know? They just kept talking about the baby and doing the ultrasound and stuff. I'd sit there and they'd be like, "Everything looks good," and then I'd leave and I'm just... scared. Like, what if something goes wrong? What if I mess up?

*glances up briefly*

My mom, she got pregnant young too, so she was like, "You'll be fine," but that didn't really... I don't know. That didn't help? And my boyfriend, he works a lot, so...

*trails off, then continues quietly*

I remember one time I was crying in the bathroom and I didn't even really know why. Just... nervous, I guess. And I couldn't tell nobody. Like, who do you even tell that to? The lady at the doctor's office? 

*slight nervous laugh*

I think I just needed somebody to like... sit and talk to me. Not tell me I'm fine. Just like... listen. And maybe tell me what's actually gonna happen, 'cause I don't really understand half the stuff they say. They use all these big words and I just nod, but then I go home and I'm confused and worried and...

*shrugs, looking uncomfortable*

Yeah. I don't know. Somebody just... paying attention would've been nice.

Okay—before you got pregnant, when you thought about looking online or using apps for fertility and getting pregnant, what did you expect you’d find? And when you actually looked, what did you come across—was it the kind of information or help you needed, or was something missing? 

(For example: did you expect medical facts, ovulation trackers, step‑by‑step guidance, or other people’s stories?)

*shifts in seat and looks down*

Um, honestly? I didn't really... I mean, I wasn't like, planning to get pregnant or nothing. It just kinda happened, you know? 

*fidgets with hands*

So I didn't really look stuff up beforehand or nothing like that. When I found out I was pregnant, my mom was like "you gotta go to the doctor," so that's what I did. I wasn't on no apps or anything.

*shrugs*

I mean, I guess if I'd known it was gonna happen, maybe I woulda looked stuff up? But I don't really... I'm not real good with computers and all that. Like, there's so much stuff on there, and I don't know what's real or what's just people talking, you know?

*looks up briefly*

Mostly I just ask my mom or the nurses here when I got questions. That's easier for me than trying to read a bunch of stuff online. Too confusing.

*pauses*

Why, is there something I shoulda been looking at?

Thanks for telling me that. Can I ask—what was the biggest difference between what you read online and what your doctor or midwife told you? Can you give an example? Did that make you trust your provider more or less, or change what you ended up doing?

*shifts in seat, picks at the edge of my sleeve*

Um... yeah, so like... I looked up stuff on my phone, you know? Facebook and whatever. And like, everybody's got stories, right? Some lady said the baby was gonna come early if I did too much walking, and another one said I *gotta* walk a lot or it won't come at all.

*laughs a little nervously*

So I was confused as hell. Then my midwife—well, the nurse actually, I think—she just said walking was fine. Normal. That I shouldn't worry about it. She didn't really... I mean, she didn't explain why everybody online says different stuff.

*shrugs*

I guess I trust her more 'cause she's the one who actually knows medical stuff or whatever. But like... it's weird when you don't know who to believe, you know? I just kinda... I dunno. I do what feels okay and hope it's right.

*looks down*

Honestly I don't really read much of the paperwork they give me anyway. The letters and stuff. It's a lot of words. I mostly just show up when they tell me to.

Did that... was that what you were asking?

Okay — if you had a health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you get ready? And when you actually asked for help, what happened?

If it helps, you can tell me:
- What the condition was (or say you didn’t have one).
- Who you expected to see (GP, OB/GYN, specialist) and what help you thought they’d give (medication changes, referrals, tests, extra monitoring, advice).
- What actually happened when you sought care — was it easy to get an appointment, did providers listen, did they change your meds or order tests, did they refer you, or did you feel dismissed?
- Any barriers (cost, wait times, travel, paperwork) and how that made you feel.

*shifts in seat and looks down*

Um, well... I had some stuff with my blood pressure before, you know? Like, it runs high sometimes. My mama's got it too.

*fidgets with hands*

I guess I thought... I dunno, I thought maybe somebody would just like, tell me what to do about it? Like, take a pill or something? I wasn't really sure who to see about it. Nobody really explained that to me before I got pregnant.

*pauses*

When I found out I was pregnant, I went to the clinic one time and they took my blood pressure and said it was okay, so... I mean, I thought we were good. But then I got another appointment letter in the mail and I couldn't... *laughs awkwardly* ...I couldn't really read all of it, you know? So I just kind of... didn't go for a while.

*looks up briefly then back down*

It's kinda far to drive into town anyway, and I was working those shifts. Hard to get time off without losing money. So yeah, I just... I guess I didn't really know what was supposed to happen? They didn't really say, like, "Destiny, you gotta do this" or explain it in a way that made sense.

*shrugs*

I'm here now though, so... that's something, right?

Thanks for telling me that — that sounds frustrating. Did you feel like you had to push hard for help, or did the clinic/team reach out and walk you through what to do?

If it's easier, you can tell me about a time they did (or didn't) help — for example, did you have to call to get an appointment or ask for tests, or did someone call you and set everything up and explain the plan?

*shifts in chair and looks down*

Um... I mean, mostly I just... I go when they tell me to go, you know? Like, I get the little card thing with the date on it, and I show up. 

*fidgets with hands*

But like, sometimes I don't really get what they're saying when they hand me stuff. There's a lot of papers and... I don't know. I just nod and be like "okay, yeah." 

*laughs a little nervously*

One time I missed something and they called me mad, and I was like "oh shoot, I forgot." But nobody really... like, they don't call ahead much and be like "hey, here's what's gonna happen." They just kind of do it when I get there.

*pauses*

I guess I'm supposed to call? But like... I don't always know what to ask for. My mama came with me once and she helped figure it out, but she's busy a lot.

*looks up briefly*

It'd be easier if somebody just... told me what to do, I guess. Like, step by step or whatever.

Before you got pregnant, what did you expect your partner’s involvement in getting ready for pregnancy to look like? And how did the way they actually helped (or didn’t) compare to what you imagined?

If it helps, you can tell me things like:
- Did you expect them to go to doctor visits, look up info, or make lifestyle changes with you?
- Were they more or less involved than you thought? In what ways?
- If they weren’t involved, what got in the way?
- How did their level of involvement make you feel?

*shifts in seat and looks down*

Yeah, um... I don't know, I guess I thought he'd, like, be around more? You know, help out and stuff. But... 

*fidgets with hands*

He don't really come to the doctor things. I asked him one time and he was like, "That's your thing," so... I just stopped asking. He works a lot, or says he does anyway. 

*shrugs*

I mean, he helped make the baby, so... I thought maybe he'd, I dunno, want to be here for it? But he's more into his friends and his truck right now. He gave me some money for stuff one time, that was good.

*looks up briefly*

It don't really bother me that much anymore. I'm used to doing things on my own anyway. My mama said men are like that, so... 

*trails off, then adds quietly*

I just wish someone would help me figure out all these forms they keep giving me at the clinic. He definitely ain't gonna read that stuff with me.

I hear you — that sounds really frustrating. Can I ask a bit more about that?

- Were there particular moments when you felt the preparation fell mostly on you? Can you tell me about one of those times?
- What do you think made you end up doing more — his reaction like “that’s your thing,” work, not knowing what to do, or something else?
- How did having that responsibility affect how you felt about yourself as a partner or about getting ready to be a parent?

Take your time — whatever you want to share is fine. If it’s easier, you can tell me one small example (like a clinic visit, shopping, or planning) and we’ll go from there.

*shifts in chair and rubs belly, looking down*

Yeah, um... I guess like, when we had to go to that first big appointment? Like, the ultrasound thing? I had to figure out where to go, I had to call 'em, fill out all that paperwork... *gestures vaguely* ...and he was just like "oh cool, let me know when it is." Showed up, looked at the screen, said it was neat, then left for work. That was it.

*picks at the armrest*

And like, the baby stuff? I'm the one who... I dunno, I looked at cribs and stuff online at work during breaks. He'd say "yeah, whatever you think is fine" but then when I asked him to help me understand what I was supposed to buy, he'd be like... tired or whatever.

*shrugs, voice quieter*

I think he just... doesn't really get it? Like, he's gonna be a dad but it doesn't feel like *real* to him yet, you know? For me it's... *touches belly again* ...it's real all the time. It's moving around in me right now.

*looks up briefly, then away*

Made me feel kinda alone, I guess. Like, this is supposed to be our baby but it feels like... my thing. My problem to figure out.

*pause*

I don't know. Maybe that's just how it is.

This might be a bit personal, so take your time — but if you’ve had a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? For example, did you think they’d do extra tests, give closer monitoring, offer counseling or support groups, or something else? And what support was actually available to you when you tried — what did doctors, nurses, or the clinic do (or not do)?

If it helps, can you say more about:
- What you hoped they'd say or do right away?
- Whether anyone talked to you about causes or how to lower the risk next time?
- If emotional support or counseling was offered, and whether that matched what you needed?
- Any barriers you ran into (costs, appointments, not being listened to, etc.)?

*shifts in seat, pulls hands into lap*

Oh... um. Yeah, well... *pauses* I had a loss before this one. It was... it was hard.

*looks down*

I didn't really know what to expect, you know? Like, I thought maybe they'd... I don't know, do something different? But honestly, when I went back to the clinic, it felt kinda like the same thing as before. They just... did the regular stuff.

*fidgets with the hem of shirt*

Nobody really sat down and like, explained why it happened or what I could do different. They just said stuff like "it happens sometimes" and... I dunno, something about chromosomes maybe? But I didn't really get it. I just nodded.

*voice gets quieter*

And counseling or whatever? Nah, nobody offered that. I mean, I was sad about it, real sad, but... *shrugs* I guess I thought that was just how it was supposed to go.

*looks up briefly*

The money thing was hard too. I had to take time off work for appointments and I couldn't afford to miss that, so... some of 'em I just didn't go to. And gas is expensive to drive to the city where the bigger clinic is.

*deflects slightly with a small laugh*

But hey, this time seems to be going okay so far, so... *trails off* Maybe it'll be different, I guess.

I'm really sorry — that sounds so hard. When you say you went back to the clinic, was there a time you felt your previous loss wasn’t being taken seriously as you tried again? 

If it helps to say more, what did they do or say that made you feel that way? How did that affect how much you trusted them going forward? Take your time — you can share as much or as little as you want.

*shifts in chair, looking down at hands*

Yeah, um... I mean, they was nice enough, but like... 

*pauses, picking at a thread on my shirt*

After I lost the baby last time, I came back like maybe six months later, and the doctor, she just... she didn't really talk about it much? Like she just said "okay, we gonna try again" and moved on to the next thing. Didn't ask me how I was doing or nothing.

*glances up briefly then back down*

And I had all these questions, you know? Like... why did it happen? Was it something I did? But she was real busy, and I... I didn't wanna be that person asking a bunch of stuff, so I just... yeah.

*shrugs*

Made me feel like maybe it wasn't no big deal to them. Like it was just something that happens. But it was a big deal to me, you know? I was sad about it.

*fidgets with hands*

So like, when I got pregnant this time, I was nervous coming back. I didn't know if they gonna care if something goes wrong again, or if they just gonna rush through it. That's why I like... I been trying to do everything right this time. Show up to appointments and stuff.

*quiet for a moment*

It's different now though. This clinic people seem more... I dunno. They ask more questions.

Okay — when you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like? Like the waiting room and staff, who you'd see, and the kind of information you thought they'd give you. And how were they actually?

If it helps, you can tell me about things like:
- Did you expect to see the same midwife or lots of different people?
- Did you think you'd get a scan or tests right away?
- Were you expecting lots of information or just basic checks?
- How did the staff act — friendly, rushed, listened to you?
- Did you feel supported, especially after your previous loss?
- Was your partner there or involved the way you thought they would be?
- Anything that surprised you or that you wished had been different?

Take your time — it's okay to answer a little at a time.

*shifts in seat and looks down at belly*

Um, yeah... I don't know, I guess I didn't really think about it that much before I came. I mean, I knew they'd, like, check on the baby and stuff, but...

*pauses*

I thought maybe it'd be like, one person? Like a doctor lady who I'd see every time? But it's been different people a lot. That's been... I don't know, kinda weird. Like, they don't know me, you know? I gotta tell 'em stuff over again.

*fidgets with hands*

And yeah, they did the scan thing right away, which was cool. I got to see the baby. That was... that was good.

*voice gets quieter*

As for like, information and stuff... they tell me things but a lot of it don't really stick. They use big words I don't get, and I'm too shy to be like "what?" *laughs nervously* So I just kinda nod and hope it was important.

The staff, they're... they're nice enough, I guess. But they seem real busy. In and out, you know?

*looks up briefly*

My boyfriend came to one appointment but he works a lot, so... it's mostly just been me.

Is there... is there something specific you're asking about?

Thanks for sharing. What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments? For example, was there anything that made you feel especially cared for or, on the flip side, ignored or uncomfortable?

*shifts in seat and looks down a bit*

Um... I guess the biggest thing was how many questions they asked me? Like, a LOT of questions. *laughs nervously* I wasn't expecting all that, you know? They wanted to know about my family and... like, medical stuff going way back. It was kinda overwhelming.

*pauses*

But I mean... there was this one nurse, I think? She was real nice. She didn't make me feel dumb when I didn't know what something was. She just... explained it different. Didn't use all the big words everybody else does.

*fidgets with hands*

The hard part was like... nobody really talks TO me, you know? They talk AT me. Just telling me what to do, what pills to take, all this stuff. And then they give me those papers... *waves hand dismissively* ...I try to read 'em but I can't hardly understand what they're saying. Half the time I just nod and hope I'm doing it right.

*shrugs*

I don't know if they were ignoring me exactly, but like... sometimes I feel like they don't really see ME. Just the pregnant belly, I guess.

Thanks — and take your time with this one. Before you had your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding the results to be like? For example, did you expect results right away or later by phone, to hear them from the sonographer, a midwife, or your GP, to get written information, or to have time to ask questions? 

And then, what actually happened? Can you tell me about how the results were given, who told you, whether you felt it was explained clearly, and how that felt compared with what you expected?

(If it helps, you can talk about small details — like whether you were left alone to wait, whether anything was said about what the tests can and can’t tell you, or whether someone offered support or next steps. If you’d rather not go into specifics, that’s totally okay.)

*shifts in seat and looks down at hands*

Um, yeah... I dunno, I guess I just thought... like, they'd do the thing and then tell me if the baby was okay or not, you know? I didn't really think about *how* it would happen. 

*picks at fingernail*

I mean, I figured somebody would just... say something. Like at the appointment. I didn't expect to wait around a bunch or get a phone call or anything fancy like that.

*pauses, then speaks more quietly*

What actually happened was... they did the ultrasound, right? And the lady doing it was real quiet the whole time. Like, not talking much. And then she was like "the doctor will talk to you about this" or something. So I'm sitting there waiting, and I'm trying to read stuff on the screen but I can't really tell what I'm looking at, and she won't say if things look good or... *trails off*

Then the doctor came in after a while and talked real fast about some numbers and things, and I was like... okay? I just kept nodding 'cause I didn't wanna sound dumb. 

*looks up briefly*

I think she explained it but I didn't really... I mean, I don't remember what she said exactly. Something about the baby being fine though? Or like, mostly fine? 

Nobody really gave me anything written down that I could look at later or nothing.

*shrugs*

It was just kinda confusing, honestly.

Yeah, that makes sense — thanks for saying that. 

Would you say there was a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you needed to make sense of the results? Like, did you feel like you got enough explanation, or were you left wanting more?

If it helps, can you tell me:
- What felt missing, if anything? (examples are fine)
- Who told you the results — and did you feel able to ask them questions?
- Did you feel like decisions were made for you, or that you had a say in what happened?

*shifts in seat and glances down at hands*

Um... yeah, I mean... *pauses* ...they explained some stuff, but like, a lot of it didn't really stick, you know? They use all these big words and I'm just like, "okay, okay," but then I get home and I'm like... what did they even say? 

*laughs nervously*

It was like... one of the nurses or somebody, they had all these papers? And they're pointing at stuff and talking real fast. I didn't wanna be like "wait, can you say that again" over and over, so I just... yeah. I just nodded.

*picks at sleeve*

I guess what I'm saying is... like, I didn't really know what was going on half the time. They'd say a number or whatever and I'm thinking "is that good? Is that bad?" But by the time I thought of asking, they were already moving on to the next thing.

And like... *trails off* ...nobody really asked me what I wanted, I guess? They just kinda told me "you need to do this" or "come back on this day," and I'm like, okay... I'll try to remember.

*looks up briefly*

It would've helped if somebody just... talked to me like a normal person, maybe? Less paper, more like... actually explaining it.
